message:
fix(mcp_missions): switch read_capped_file to streaming bounded read (ft-up6u2)

`read_capped_file` previously used a two-stage defense against
oversize mission-file DoS:

1. `std::fs::metadata(path).len() > max_bytes` — early rejection
   based on the stat result.
2. `std::fs::read_to_string(path)` — full-file slurp into a String,
   followed by a post-read `raw.len() > max_bytes` check.

Stage 1 is dodgeable on any filesystem that misreports size (procfs-
style pseudo files that return 0 on stat, networked FS with
untrusted stat, a TOCTOU swap between stat and open). Once stage 1
is bypassed, stage 2 has already allocated the full file contents
into memory — which is exactly the OOM shape the bead is meant to
prevent. A multi-GiB `.ft/mission/active.json` passed via a hostile
repo or a worker-lab scratch mount would allocate the full content
in RAM before the length check rejected it.

Fix: stage 2 is now a streaming bounded read:

- `std::fs::File::open(path)` instead of read_to_string (so we
  control the I/O layer).
- `file.take(max_bytes.saturating_add(1)).read_to_end(&mut buf)` —
  Read::take is the correct DoS defense: it caps the I/O layer
  itself. The reader stops after max_bytes+1 regardless of actual
  file size, so the peak allocation is bounded at max_bytes+1
  regardless of what stat reports or how the filesystem behaves.
- `saturating_add(1)` so we can distinguish "exactly at the cap"
  from "over the cap" without overflowing u64. If the reader
  consumed max_bytes+1 bytes, the file was at least one byte larger
  than the cap — reject with `oversize_error_code`.
- `Vec::with_capacity(max_bytes.min(1 << 20) as usize)` — pre-reserve
  up to 1 MiB to avoid thrashing small reads, but never more than
  the cap (so a small max_bytes doesn't force a big up-front
  allocation).
- UTF-8 validation via `String::from_utf8(buf)` replaces the implicit
  validation read_to_string gave us. Returns `read_error_code` on
  invalid UTF-8 because a non-UTF-8 byte sequence in a JSON file is
  a structural error, not an oversize one.
- Metadata stat remains for early rejection of the common hostile-
  repo case (multi-GiB file detected on open without any I/O).
  Stat failure falls through deliberately — the streaming reader in
  stage 2 is the real defense, and network/procfs mounts where stat
  is unreliable should not hard-fail at the early stage.

Error messages updated to reflect the new semantics ("streaming
reader stopped after reading N bytes" instead of "read N bytes").

No API or caller-contract change: function signature, return type,
and error codes are identical. The two error codes still mean the
same thing ("file too large" vs. "other I/O problem").

Inline comments on both stages spell out the threat model (stat
lies, TOCTOU swap) and the defense (Read::take as an I/O-layer cap)
so future readers understand why the apparently-more-verbose
approach is correct.
